# Serviceable Addressable Market: How to Calculate SAM

Learn how to calculate your serviceable addressable market (SAM) with bottom-up and top-down methods, free data sources, and investor-ready validation tips.

9 min readProspeo Team

## Serviceable Addressable Market: How to Calculate It and Find the Data

Your co-founder just pinged you: "We need the market size for the deck by Friday." You open a browser, type in your niche, and find nothing useful - just analyst PDFs behind $5,000 paywalls and blog posts recycling the same vague definitions. So you default to the lazy "1% of a billion-dollar market" slide that makes investors cringe.

You don't need a Gartner subscription. You need a method, a few free government datasets, and a B2B database to count real companies. Here's exactly how to size your serviceable addressable market - and make the number airtight.

Quick version: SAM is the slice of your total addressable market you can actually reach and sell to today. Calculate it bottom-up: count target companies matching your ICP, multiply by your average contract value. Use free Census data plus NAICS codes for industry-level counts, a B2B database for company-level ICP filtering, and validate by checking that your top-down and bottom-up estimates stay within 3x of each other.

## What Is SAM?

     "text": "Addressable Markets: TAM, SAM & SOM Guide (2026)

# Addressable Markets: TAM, SAM & SOM Guide (2026)

Learn how to calculate addressable markets - TAM, SAM, and SOM - with bottom-up methods investors trust. Formulas, examples, and free tools inside.

12 min readProspeo Team

## Addressable Markets: TAM, SAM, and SOM (Plus How to Calculate Them)

Investors spend 3 minutes and 44 seconds on your entire pitch deck. Your TAM slide gets a fraction of that - and if it opens with a trillion-dollar number pulled from a Gartner report, those seconds end with an eye roll. Inaccurate addressable market estimates cost businesses up to $15M annually. The difference between a credible market slide and a lazy one isn't the size of the number. It's whether you can defend how you got there.

## The Quick Version

- TAM = total revenue if you sold to every possible customer. SAM = the slice you can actually reach. SOM = what you'll realistically capture.
- Investors trust bottom-up sizing over top-down. Always lead with bottom-up.
- SOM benchmarks that won't get you laughed out of the room: 1% of SAM in Year 1, 3% in Year 2, 5% in Year 3.
- Cross-check rule of thumb: if your top-down and bottom-up estimates land within 2x of each other, your assumptions are healthy. If they're 10x apart, something's broken.
- "We only need 1% of the market" is the fastest way to lose investor credibility. It dodges the only question that matters: why you win.

## What "Addressable Markets" Actually Means

## Major Takeaways

What is the difference between TAM, SAM, and SOM?

TAM is the total revenue opportunity if you had 100% market share. SAM is the portion you can realistically reach with your current product and go-to-market. SOM is the portion you can actually capture in the near term given your resources, competition, and execution capacity.

Why do most B2B companies get market sizing wrong?

They use top-down analyst reports that overcount (including companies that are not actually buyers), skip the SAM step entirely, and never validate their TAM against real company data. The result is a market size that impresses board decks but does not help sales teams prioritize.

How do you turn market sizing into actionable pipeline?

Build your TAM as a live database of actual companies, not a revenue number from an analyst report. Score every company against your ICP criteria to get your SAM. Filter by buying signals and competitive presence"

# TAM, SAM, SOM: A Practical Market Sizing Framework

April 16, 2026 · 9 min read

## Why Most Market Sizing Is Wrong (and Why It Still Matters)

Every investor pitch and board presentation includes a market sizing slide, and most of them are wrong. The typical approach -- pull a number from a Gartner or IDC report, claim you will capture 1% of a billion-dollar market -- tells you nothing useful about your actual opportunity. It is a vanity exercise dressed up as strategy.

Yet market sizing done correctly is one of the most valuable strategic exercises a company can undertake. A rigorous bottom-up analysis forces you to define exactly who your customer is, how many of them exist, what they currently pay for the problem you solve, and what share of their spending you can realistically capture. This exercise shapes your pricing, your sales model, your hiring plan, and your fundraising targets. The number itself matters less than the thinking that produces it.

## Top-Down vs. Bottom-Up: When to Use Each Method

Top-down sizing starts with a published market figure and narrows it by applying filters. If the global CRM market is USD 80 billion, and you target mid-market companies in Europe, you might estimate the European mid-market segment at 12% of the total, yielding a TAM of USD 9.6 billion. This method is fast and useful for initial sanity checks, but it hides critical assumptions. What counts"

Most TAM calculators give you fantasy numbers. Here’s why yours probably overestimate by 300%.

## The $70 Billion Problem with Generic Market Sizing

Picture this scenario. You’ve built a revolutionary sensor for industrial automation. Your engineering team spent two years perfecting the technology. Now you need to convince leadership that the market opportunity justifies scaling production.

You fire up a standard TAM/SAM/SOM calculator, plug in some industry data, and get a number that looks impressive: $2.3 billion total addressable market. Leadership approves aggressive expansion plans. Six months later, your sales team has contacted 500 prospects and closed exactly three deals.

The market sizing was wrong. Catastrophically wrong.

This scenario plays out daily across technical B2B companies. With U.S. B2B technology sales projected to pass $70 billion by 2025 [1], the stakes for accurate market sizing have never been higher. Yet most companies rely on generic frameworks that ignore the fundamental complexity of technical markets.

Key Takeaway: Generic TAM/SAM/SOM calculators fail in technical B2B markets because they ignore application-specific requirements and complex buyer behavior.

Ready to transform your customer research approach? Join our waitlist to access proven methodologies that technical buyers trust.

The problem isn’t just inaccuracy—it’s strategic misdirection."

     "text": "SAM (serviceable addressable market) | GrowPanel

## Table of contents

# SAM (serviceable addressable market)

---

SAM is the fastest way to find out whether your growth plan is even possible. If your SAM can't support your target ARR without implausible market share, the fix isn't "try harder"—it's changing your segment, pricing, product scope, or go-to-market.

SAM (Serviceable Addressable Market) is the portion of the market you can realistically serve with your current product and go-to-market constraints—typically defined by who you sell to, where you can sell, and what you can actually support and deliver.

TAM sets context, SAM sets what's feasible, and SOM sets what you can realistically win on your current plan.

## What SAM actually includes

Founders often treat SAM as "TAM but smaller." In practice, SAM is a capability and focus statement:

- Key takeaways
- Customer definition: your ICP (industry, size, complexity) and buying center.
- Geography: where you can sell/support today (language, time zones, payments, taxes).
- Product readiness: must-have features for that segment (security, permissions, workflows).
- Compliance and risk: regulatory requirements (SOC 2, HIPAA, GDPR), procurement realities.
- Integrations and ecosystem: systems you must connect to (ERP/accounting/SSO).
- Support and delivery model: onboarding burden, implementation time, service capacity.
- Price feasibility: what that segment can pay given your value and competition.

This is why SAM"

Market sizing for B2B tech companies is the process of estimating the realistic revenue opportunity available to your product within a defined market. Done properly, it produces three numbers: your Total Addressable Market (TAM), your Serviceable Addressable Market (SAM), and your Serviceable Obtainable Market (SOM). Most companies get the first two roughly right and completely ignore the third, which is the only one that actually matters for planning.

The goal is not to produce a large number for a pitch deck. The goal is to understand whether the opportunity justifies the investment, where to focus first, and what realistic growth looks like over a three to five year horizon.

### Key Takeaways

- TAM, SAM and SOM serve different purposes. TAM sets context, SAM defines focus, and SOM is the only number that should drive resource allocation and planning.

# EN 50600 (DC)

The European standard EN 50600 provides comprehensive guidelines for the planning, construction, and operation of data centers, focusing on power supply, air conditioning, and security infrastructure. TÜV NORD experts support you in designing your data center to be future-proof and state-of-the-art.

## Fundamentals and significance of European standard EN 50600 for data centers

Appropriate measures must be taken to ensure the physical security and availability of IT infrastructures. In this context, the European standard EN 50600 offers a holistic approach to the planning, construction and operation of data centers.

It provides planners, installers and operators of data centers with comprehensive guidelines in the form of various requirements, particularly with regard to power supply, air conditioning and security infrastructure.

Our experts provide comprehensive services to support you in planning, operating or expanding your data center in a future-proof manner based on EN 50600 in accordance with the current state of the art.

EN 50600 Data Center Expert (TÜV®) Training

EN 50600 is a European series of standards for data centers that uses a holistic approach to provide comprehensive specifications for the planning, construction and operation of data centers.

# Services

Find below some of the requested and successfully offered services from B2E. All our services can be called on a modular basis. All services are highly trackable and come with comprehensive documentation.

Market Analysis and Assistance

- Market Research
- Competitive Analysis
- Identifying potential business partners
- Go-to-market Analysis
- Necessary certifications (WEEE, CE, TÜV, Grüner Punkt, FCC, ROHS)
- Market entry barriers (EORI, custom-forms)
- Target definition
- Business planning (GfK, ITscope, ProSeller)

- Business Development
- External Sales Management
- Project Management
- Partner/Reseller Acquisition
- Channel Development
- Actively supporting the distribution channel
- Product forecasting and business planning

- External Marketing Management
- Partner Program Development (CRM)
- Performance of Marketing Strategies
- Affiliate Partner Solutions
- Campaign Development
- Product Positioning & Messaging
- Organizing Road Shows or Trade Shows
- Localization of Press Releases
- Placement of Press Releases
- Budget planning and handling
- Localizations (press releases, packaging, brochures, white papers)
- Website development or localization
- Implementation of a local webshop
- Service model development (24/7, on-site, etc.)
- Sample-/ demo- and reviewers-handling
- Optimizing transport, handling and customs
- Local company postal-address and phone number
- Native web search optimization capability (SEO)
- Amazon acc"

content: {"claims":[{"claim":"SOM benchmark heuristic cited as 1% of SAM in year one, rising to 3% and 5% in years two and three — an adjacent metric to SAM that could be conflated with the serviceable share.","number":1,"unit":"%","sourceTitle":"Addressable Markets: TAM, SAM & SOM Guide (2026)","url":"https://prospeo.io/s/addressable-markets","excerpt":"SOM benchmarks that won't get you laughed out of the room: 1% of SAM in Year 1, 3% in Year 2, 5% in Year 3.","tags":["definition","base-rate"]},{"claim":"Cross-check heuristic: top-down and bottom-up market estimates should land within 2x of each other to be considered healthy; 10x apart signals a broken model.","number":2,"unit":"x","sourceTitle":"Addressable Markets: TAM, SAM & SOM Guide (2026)","url":"https://prospeo.io/s/addressable-markets","excerpt":"Cross-check rule of thumb: if your top-down and bottom-up estimates land within 2x of each other, your assumptions are healthy. If they're 10x apart, something's broken.","tags":["structure","base-rate"]},{"claim":"Inaccurate addressable-market estimates are claimed to cost businesses up to $15M annually.","number":15,"unit":"$M/year","sourceTitle":"Addressable Markets: TAM, SAM & SOM Guide (2026)","url":"https://prospeo.io/s/addressable-markets","excerpt":"Inaccurate addressable market estimates cost businesses up to $15M annually.","tags":["base-rate"]},{"claim":"Illustrative top-down SAM example narrows a global market to a regional/segment share of 12% of TAM — an order of magnitude below the 45-65% serviceable-factor range assumed for the model.","number":12,"unit":"%","sourceTitle":"TAM, SAM, SOM: A Practical Market Sizing Framework | Scalarly Blog","url":"https://scalarly.com/blog/tam-sam-som-market-sizing-framework/","excerpt":"If the global CRM market is USD 80 billion, and you target mid-market companies in Europe, you might estimate the European mid-market segment at 12% of the total, yielding a TAM of USD 9.6 billion.","tags":["definition","structure"]},{"claim":"Generic TAM/SAM/SOM calculators in technical B2B/industrial markets are alleged to overestimate market size by roughly 300%.","number":300,"unit":"%","sourceTitle":"Niche B2B Market Sizing Guide: TAM SAM SOM Framework for Technical Sales Team - GrowthBeaver","url":"https://growthbeaver.com/tam-sam-som-calculator-niche-b2b-markets/","excerpt":"Most TAM calculators give you fantasy numbers. Here's why yours probably overestimate by 300%.","tags":["base-rate","structure"]},{"claim":"U.S. B2B technology sales were projected to exceed $70 billion by 2025, used as context for technical B2B market-sizing stakes.","number":70,"unit":"$B","sourceTitle":"Niche B2B Market Sizing Guide: TAM SAM SOM Framework for Technical Sales Team - GrowthBeaver","url":"https://growthbeaver.com/tam-sam-som-calculator-niche-b2b-markets/","excerpt":"With U.S. B2B technology sales projected to pass $70 billion by 2025 [1], the stakes for accurate market sizing have never been higher.","tags":["demand","base-rate"]},{"claim":"EU ecodesign regulation sets specific quantitative PSU efficiency and power-factor minimums for servers/storage products (e.g., 90-96% efficiency thresholds by load level from 2023), evidencing a hard technical compliance gate for EU market entry.","number":96,"unit":"%","sourceTitle":"ANNEX II Ecodesign requirements (EU 2019/424)","url":"https://webgate.ec.europa.eu/reqs2/public/v2/requirement/auxi/eu/32019R0424_stdecoserver_annex_2.pdf/","excerpt":"Minimum PSU efficiency Minimum power factor % of rated load 10 % 20 % 50 % 100 % 50 % Multi output — 90 % 94 % 91 % 0,95 Single output 90 % 94 % 96 % 91 % 0,95","tags":["regulation"]},{"claim":"EN 50600 is a European standard covering planning, construction, and operation of data centers, focused on power supply, air conditioning and security infrastructure — a certification gate referenced qualitatively in the node's derivation.","number":null,"unit":null,"sourceTitle":"EN 50600 – European standard for data centers | TÜV NORD","url":"https://www.tuev-nord.de/en/services/auditing-and-certification/en-50600/","excerpt":"The European standard EN 50600 provides comprehensive guidelines for the planning, construction, and operation of data centers, focusing on power supply, air conditioning, and security infrastructure.","tags":["regulation"]},{"claim":"A new EU Delegated Regulation (2024/1364), effective May 2024, establishes the first phase of a common Union rating scheme for data centres — a regulatory layer not mentioned in the node's cited cert stack.","number":null,"unit":null,"sourceTitle":"EUR-Lex - 02024R1364-20240517 - EN","url":"https://eur-lex.europa.eu/eli/reg_del/2024/1364","excerpt":"Commission Delegated Regulation (EU) 2024/1364 of 14 March 2024 on the first phase of the establishment of a common Union rating scheme for data centres","tags":["regulation"]},{"claim":"The EU Energy Efficiency Directive (2024/1791), in force since October 2023, imposes disclosure of sustainability KPIs and Waste Heat Reuse requirements specifically on datacenter operators/owners, an additional regulatory burden beyond ecodesign/EN50600/CE-TÜV.","number":null,"unit":null,"sourceTitle":"The EU's Energy Efficiency Directive and Its Impact on Datacenters","url":"https://www.cov.com/-/media/files/corporate/publications/2025/04/the-eus-energy-efficiency-directive-and-its-impact-on-datacenters.pdf","excerpt":"It also imposes specific requirements on operators and owners of datacenters (disclosure of KPIs; WHR requirements)","tags":["regulation"]},{"claim":"A market-entry service provider lists a broader certification stack for EU hardware go-to-market than the node cites: WEEE, CE, TÜV, Grüner Punkt, FCC, ROHS, plus customs barriers like EORI, beyond just EN 50600/ecodesign/CE-TÜV.","number":null,"unit":null,"sourceTitle":"Bridge to Europe - Services","url":"https://www.b-to-e.com/services/","excerpt":"Necessary certifications (WEEE, CE, TÜV, Grüner Punkt, FCC, ROHS) Market entry barriers (EORI, custom-forms)","tags":["regulation","execution"]},{"claim":"A generic market-sizing calculator defaults SOM to a 10% capture rate of SAM, framing 'realistic startup capture' as 1-5% in year one — again a SOM (not SAM) benchmark, far below the 45-65% serviceable-factor range.","number":10,"unit":"%","sourceTitle":"TAM SAM SOM Calculator 2026 | Free Market Size Analysis | IdeaProof","url":"https://ideaproof.io/calculators/market-size","excerpt":"SOM · Obtainable — SAM × 10% capture ... Realistic startup capture is 1–5% in y[ear one]","tags":["base-rate","structure"]},{"claim":"SAM is defined as a capability/focus statement encompassing customer definition, geography, product readiness, compliance/regulatory requirements, integrations, support model, and price feasibility — not a single fixed percentage of TAM.","number":null,"unit":null,"sourceTitle":"SAM (serviceable addressable market) | GrowPanel","url":"https://growpanel.io/academy/sam/","excerpt":"Compliance and risk: regulatory requirements (SOC 2, HIPAA, GDPR), procurement realities.","tags":["definition","regulation"]},{"claim":"Investors are said to distrust top-down '1% of a billion-dollar market' claims and prefer bottom-up sizing — reinforcing that the serviceableFactor node's top-down 0.55 assumption lacks the bottom-up support investors/analysts expect.","number":null,"unit":null,"sourceTitle":"Addressable Markets: TAM, SAM & SOM Guide (2026)","url":"https://prospeo.io/s/addressable-markets","excerpt":"Investors trust bottom-up sizing over top-down. Always lead with bottom-up.","tags":["structure","execution"]}],"tensions":[{"text":"The only quantified analogues found for 'serviceable/obtainable share' in B2B markets are SOM benchmarks of 1-10% (year-one capture) and an illustrative SAM example of 12% of TAM — both far below the node's 0.45-0.65 serviceableFactor range, raising the risk that 0.55 conflates a generous SAM definition with what search evidence treats as a narrower, single-digit-to-low-teens obtainable/serviceable slice.","nodes":["serviceableFactor"]},{"text":"The node's derivation cites only three EU certification regimes (EN 50600, ecodesign 2019/424, CE/TÜV) as the qualitative basis for the sub-1.0 haircut, but search results surface a materially larger and more recent regulatory stack (WEEE, FCC, ROHS, EORI customs, the 2024 EU data-centre rating scheme, and the Energy Efficiency Directive's KPI/WHR disclosure rules) — suggesting the true regulatory-reach barrier could be stronger than represented, pushing serviceableFactor toward or below the 0.45 lower bound rather than centering at 0.55.","nodes":["serviceableFactor"]},{"text":"No search result produced any bottom-up, named-account or channel-coverage model quantifying reachable share for B2B data-center hardware, directly matching the node's own admission that no public SAM/TAM benchmark exists — this validates the 'needs-source' status but also means the specific point estimate of 0.55 (versus, e.g., 0.45 or 0.65) remains unanchored to any external data found.","nodes":["serviceableFactor"]}],"absences":["No published
